correctAnswer: Both momentum AND kinetic energy are conserved
acceptableAlternatives: elastic collision conserves both p and KE
quizQuestion: In an elastic collision, what quantities are conserved?
explanation: Elastic collisions conserve both: (1) total momentum (p_total,i = p_total,f) AND (2) total kinetic energy (KE_total,i = KE_total,f). This gives two equations for a two-body collision, which (in 1D) fully determines both final velocities. For equal masses in an elastic collision: the velocities simply exchange — the moving object stops and the stationary one moves.
visualDescription: Billiard ball colliding elastically: first ball stops, second ball moves at first ball's original speed
distractors: Only momentum is conserved
Only kinetic energy is conserved
Neither is conserved
Total energy and speed are conserved
Momentum and angular momentum only
Total force and momentum are conserved
Kinetic energy and potential energy are conserved
Speed only is conserved (not direction)

correctAnswer: Some kinetic energy converts to thermal energy, sound, or deformation
acceptableAlternatives: KE lost to heat, deformation, sound in inelastic collisions
quizQuestion: In an inelastic collision, where does the 'lost' kinetic energy go?
explanation: In inelastic collisions, some KE converts to: thermal energy (internal energy from deformation), sound energy, or deformation energy (permanent shape changes). Total energy is still conserved — only the mechanical portion decreases. Car crash crumple zones intentionally maximize deformation to dissipate KE and protect passengers.
visualDescription: Two clay balls colliding with heat and sound symbols indicating energy conversion from KE
distractors: It disappears (energy is not conserved)
It converts to potential energy
It converts to momentum
It transfers to the environment as gravitational PE
It is stored in the spring between objects
It converts to chemical energy
It is radiated as light
It is absorbed by the surrounding air as pressure

correctAnswer: The center of mass moves at constant velocity (if no external net force)
acceptableAlternatives: CM velocity is constant with no external force
quizQuestion: When two objects collide (with no external net force), how does the center of mass of the system move?
explanation: The center of mass continues to move at constant velocity v_cm = p_total/m_total throughout the collision, because the total momentum is conserved. Internal forces (the collision forces) do not change the CM velocity. Even in an explosion, the CM of the fragments continues moving at the pre-explosion velocity. This is a powerful constraint for analyzing collisions.
visualDescription: Two colliding objects with the CM marked as a dot moving in a straight line at constant velocity through the collision
distractors: The CM stops during the collision
The CM accelerates in the direction of the larger mass
The CM speed equals the average of the two speeds
The CM oscillates between the objects
The CM always moves toward the heavier object
The CM velocity equals zero during collision
The CM reverses direction in elastic collisions
The CM velocity equals the velocity of the more massive object

correctAnswer: External net force changes the system's total momentum
acceptableAlternatives: momentum is NOT conserved when external forces act
quizQuestion: Under what condition is conservation of momentum NOT valid for a system?
explanation: Conservation of momentum requires zero net external force. If ΣF_external ≠ 0, then Δp_total = ΣF_external × Δt ≠ 0. A common example: a ball bouncing off a wall has its momentum changed by the normal force from the wall (external force). During a short collision, external forces (gravity, normal) are often neglected because the collision forces are much larger — this is called the 'impulse approximation.'
visualDescription: A system diagram with external forces labeled as changing the total momentum arrow
distractors: Momentum is never conserved
Momentum is not conserved when masses are unequal
Momentum is not conserved in elastic collisions
Momentum is not conserved when speed is high
Momentum is not conserved if the collision is brief
Momentum is not conserved when KE changes
Momentum is conserved only for identical masses
Momentum is not conserved in 2D collisions
